questions.stem: A client with adrenal insufficiency has vomiting, weakness, blood pressure 82/48 mmHg, and sodium 128 mEq/L. Which provider prescription should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Administer IV hydrocortisone and isotonic fluids.
Restrict oral fluids and monitor weight.
Hold corticosteroids until the morning cortisol level is drawn.
Teach the client about low-sodium foods.

questions.rationale: Hypotension, hyponatremia, vomiting, and weakness in a client with adrenal insufficiency suggest adrenal crisis. The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse. Delaying treatment for further testing can be life-threatening.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "sodium 128 mEq/L" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "sodium 128 mEq/L" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "Teach the client about low-sodium foods" misses the bedside risk. The cue "The priority is rapid steroid replacement and volume support to prevent circulatory collapse" points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Suspected adrenal crisis requires steroids and isotonic fluids right away.

questions.stem: A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure of 208/110 mmHg. Which nursing action is the priority?

options.text: Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention.
Lay the client flat and increase the room temperature.
Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes.
Offer oral fluids and reassess in 30 minutes.

questions.rationale: Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered by bladder distention. The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away. Delay increases the risk of stroke or seizure.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of neurologic safety assessment. The stem cue "The nurse should sit the client upright to lower blood pressure and remove the triggering stimulus right away" makes neurologic deterioration the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: "Ask the client to rest quietly until the headache passes" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Severe hypertension and headache in a client with a high spinal cord injury suggest autonomic dysreflexia, often triggered" raises concern for neurologic deterioration, so neurologic safety assessment is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The option can leave neurologic deterioration untreated because it treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. "Sit the client upright and assess for bladder distention" fits the safer response to the cue "A client with a T4 spinal cord injury suddenly reports a pounding headache and has a blood pressure".

questions.takeaway: Autonomic dysreflexia is a remove-the-trigger emergency, often starting with sitting the client up and checking the bladder.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with a short leg cast reports increasing pain that is not relieved by opioids. The toes are pale and the client reports numbness. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Notify the provider immediately about possible compartment syndrome.
Apply ice over the cast and reassess after the next pain dose.
Elevate the leg above the level of the heart.
Loosen the cast with scissors at the bedside.

questions.rationale: Pain out of proportion, pallor, and numbness suggest compartment syndrome, which can threaten tissue viability and limb function. Immediate provider notification and urgent evaluation are needed. Comfort-only measures or excessive elevation can worsen ischemia or delay definitive treatment.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "Pain that outpaces the injury plus pallor or numbness under a cast should trigger concern for compartment syndrome" suggests medication toxicity, so "Apply ice over the cast and reassess after the next pain dose" would not correct the active problem. Medication safety depends on checking contraindications, labs, and adverse effects before giving or continuing the drug.
distractorRationales.c: "Elevate the leg above the level of the heart" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Pain that outpaces the injury plus pallor or numbness under a cast should trigger concern for compartment syndrome" raises concern for medication toxicity, so medication-safety intervention is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Loosen the cast with scissors at the bedside" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before medication-safety intervention.

questions.takeaway: Pain that outpaces the injury plus pallor or numbness under a cast should trigger concern for compartment syndrome.

questions.stem: A laboring client receives an epidural and 5 minutes later becomes pale and dizzy. Blood pressure is 78/42 mmHg and the fetal heart rate shows recurrent late decelerations. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Reposition the client and increase the IV fluid rate.
Encourage the client to push with the next contraction.
Lower the head of the bed and leave the client flat.
Document an expected epidural response and reassess later.

questions.rationale: Maternal hypotension after epidural analgesia can reduce uteroplacental perfusion and trigger late decelerations. The nurse should reposition the client and support circulating volume immediately. Delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk.

distractorRationales.b: "Encourage the client to push with the next contraction" commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. The cue "Delaying action increases fetal hypoxia risk" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before diagnostic reassurance.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of circulation and perfusion support. The stem cue "Blood pressure is 78/42 mmHg and the fetal heart rate shows recurrent late decelerations" makes circulation or perfusion the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses circulation or perfusion; "Document an expected epidural response and reassess later" moves care toward routine observation before circulation and perfusion support.

questions.takeaway: Maternal hypotension after epidural should be corrected quickly to restore fetal perfusion.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: A client with acute kidney injury has a potassium level of 6.8 mEq/L and peaked T waves on the cardiac monitor. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Prepare to administer IV calcium gluconate.
Encourage the client to increase oral fluids.
Place the client on a potassium-restricted diet.
Repeat the potassium level in 4 hours.

questions.rationale: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes places the client at immediate risk for lethal dysrhythmias. IV calcium gluconate stabilizes the cardiac membrane while other therapies work to lower serum potassium. Dietary teaching and delayed reassessment do not address the urgent electrical instability.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "8 mEq/L and peaked T waves on the cardiac monitor" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Encourage the client to increase oral fluids" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: "Place the client on a potassium-restricted diet" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "8 mEq/L and peaked T waves on the cardiac monitor" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: "Repeat the potassium level in 4 hours" moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. The cue "8 mEq/L and peaked T waves on the cardiac monitor" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before ambulation, intake, or discharge teaching.

questions.takeaway: Hyperkalemia plus peaked T waves is a cardiac emergency: stabilize the myocardium first.

questions.stem: A client taking lithium for bipolar disorder has vomiting, coarse hand tremors, and increasing confusion after several days of diarrhea. Which action should the nurse take first?

options.text: Hold the lithium and notify the provider.
Give the lithium with crackers to reduce stomach upset.
Teach the client to double the next dose.
Encourage the client to restrict sodium intake further.

questions.rationale: Vomiting, worsening tremor, and confusion after fluid loss suggest lithium toxicity. The nurse should stop further doses and notify the provider for prompt evaluation. Dehydration and sodium loss can raise lithium levels, so more restriction or additional dosing would be unsafe.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward premature attribution instead of medication-safety intervention. The stem cue "A client taking lithium for bipolar disorder has vomiting, coarse hand tremors" makes medication toxicity the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave medication toxicity untreated because it uses teaching before the physiologic risk is controlled. "Hold the lithium and notify the provider" fits the safer response to the cue "confusion after fluid loss suggest lithium toxicity".
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses medication toxicity; "Encourage the client to restrict sodium intake further" moves care toward routine care before medication-safety intervention.

questions.takeaway: GI loss plus confusion and coarse tremor on lithium should be treated as toxicity until proven otherwise.

questions.stem: The RN is assigning care for a group of postoperative clients. Which task is appropriate to delegate to an experienced LPN/LVN?

options.text: Reinforce teaching already provided about incentive spirometry.
Perform the initial assessment of a client returning from PACU.
Develop the nursing care plan for a new admission.
Interpret a change in the client's telemetry rhythm.

questions.rationale: An experienced LPN or LVN may reinforce established teaching and perform focused care for stable clients, depending on policy. The initial postoperative assessment, care planning, and rhythm interpretation require RN-level assessment and clinical judgment. Safe delegation depends on stability, predictability, and the need for judgment.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it commits to a competing cause before checking the dangerous pattern. "Reinforce teaching already provided about incentive spirometry" fits the safer response to the cue "Safe delegation depends on stability, predictability".
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Reinforce teaching already provided about incentive spirometry" fits the safer response to the cue "Safe delegation depends on stability, predictability".
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "Interpret a change in the client's telemetry rhythm" moves care toward routine care before bedside safety action.

questions.takeaway: Delegate stable, predictable care and reinforcement to the LPN or LVN, not initial assessment or interpretation.
questions.editorial_notes: source-rotation: selected from the current ChapAI NCLEX draft library with recent-family avoidance and curated-seed preference.

questions.stem: In the postanesthesia care unit, a client develops rising end-tidal CO2, muscle rigidity, and a rapidly increasing temperature. Which action is the priority?

options.text: Prepare to administer dantrolene and call the rapid response team.
Cover the client with warm blankets to prevent shivering.
Encourage the client to cough and deep-breathe.
Delay action until laboratory confirmation is available.

questions.rationale: Rising end-tidal CO2, rigidity, and hyperthermia after anesthesia suggest malignant hyperthermia, a life-threatening emergency. Dantrolene and immediate escalation are critical. Waiting for lab confirmation or focusing on comfort measures delays definitive treatment.

distractorRationales.b: The safer response addresses clinical safety risk; "Cover the client with warm blankets to prevent shivering" moves care toward diagnostic reassurance before bedside safety action.
distractorRationales.c: The option can leave clinical safety risk untreated because it moves to activity, intake, or discharge planning too early. "Prepare to administer dantrolene and call the rapid response team" fits the safer response to the cue "hyperthermia after anesthesia suggest malignant hyperthermia, a life-threatening emergency".
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward delayed assessment instead of bedside safety action. The stem cue "hyperthermia after anesthesia suggest malignant hyperthermia, a life-threatening emergency" makes clinical safety risk the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: Suspected malignant hyperthermia demands immediate dantrolene and emergency escalation.

questions.stem: A client is admitted with suspected bacterial meningitis. Which infection-control measure should the nurse implement first?

options.text: Initiate droplet precautions.
Place the client in airborne isolation.
Use reverse isolation to protect the client.
Delay precautions until a lumbar puncture confirms the diagnosis.

questions.rationale: Suspected bacterial meningitis requires prompt droplet precautions to reduce transmission risk. The nurse should not wait for confirmation before initiating appropriate isolation. Airborne or reverse isolation do not match the primary transmission risk for typical bacterial meningitis.

distractorRationales.b: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "Airborne or reverse isolation do not match the primary transmission risk for typical bacterial meningitis" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "Start droplet precautions right away when bacterial meningitis is suspected" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: "Delay precautions until a lumbar puncture confirms the diagnosis" treats active deterioration as something safe to watch. The cue "Suspected bacterial meningitis requires prompt droplet precautions to reduce transmission risk" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before routine observation.

questions.takeaway: Start droplet precautions right away when bacterial meningitis is suspected.

questions.stem: A client receiving chemotherapy has an absolute neutrophil count of 400/mm3. Which nursing action is most appropriate?

options.text: Avoid fresh flowers and raw fruits brought from outside.
Take a rectal temperature every 4 hours.
Limit oral care to once each shift to avoid bleeding.
Encourage the client to visit the crowded day room to reduce isolation.

questions.rationale: An absolute neutrophil count of 400/mm3 indicates severe neutropenia and a high risk for infection. Reducing exposure to environmental organisms, including flowers and unwashed fresh produce, is appropriate. Rectal temperatures and unnecessary crowd exposure increase infection risk rather than protecting the client.

distractorRationales.b: "Take a rectal temperature every 4 hours" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Rectal temperatures and unnecessary crowd exposure increase infection risk rather than protecting the client" raises concern for infection progression, so infection or sepsis escalation is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward premature activity instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "An absolute neutrophil count of 400/mm3 indicates severe neutropenia and a high risk for infection" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "An absolute neutrophil count of 400/mm3 indicates severe neutropenia and a high risk for infection" makes infection progression the active safety concern.

questions.takeaway: With severe neutropenia, the nurse should lower exposure to infection sources rather than increase invasive or crowd contact.

questions.stem: A newborn is 18 hours old and is noted to have yellow discoloration of the sclera and chest. Which response by the nurse is best?

options.text: Report the early jaundice because jaundice in the first 24 hours is concerning.
Reassure the parent that all newborn jaundice is expected.
Encourage the parent to place the newborn in direct sunlight.
Wait until the newborn is 48 hours old before documenting the finding.

questions.rationale: Jaundice appearing in the first 24 hours of life is not considered normal transitional jaundice and should be reported for prompt evaluation. Early jaundice can signal hemolysis or other pathologic causes. Reassurance or delay risks missing rising bilirubin levels that may need treatment.

distractorRationales.b: The cue "Jaundice appearing in the first 24 hours of life is not considered normal transitional jaundice and should be" suggests circulation or perfusion, so "Reassure the parent that all newborn jaundice is expected" would not correct the active problem. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.
distractorRationales.c: "Encourage the parent to place the newborn in direct sunlight" answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. The cue "Jaundice appearing in the first 24 hours of life is not considered normal transitional jaundice and should be" raises concern for circulation or perfusion, so circulation and perfusion support is needed before routine care.
distractorRationales.d: "Wait until the newborn is 48 hours old before documenting the finding" misses the bedside risk. The cue "A newborn is 18 hours old and is noted to have yellow discoloration of the sclera and chest" points to circulation or perfusion. Perfusion problems require rapid assessment and stabilization before comfort measures or delayed provider follow-up.

questions.takeaway: Jaundice in the first 24 hours is a report-now finding in the newborn.

questions.stem: Two days after a cesarean birth, a client has uterine tenderness, foul-smelling lochia, and a temperature of 101.8 F (38.8 C). Which complication should the nurse suspect?

options.text: Endometritis.
Uterine inversion.
Postpartum blues.
Expected afterpains.

questions.rationale: Fever, uterine tenderness, and foul-smelling lochia after birth are classic findings of endometritis. The cesarean birth increases infection risk, making this complication especially important to recognize quickly. Uterine inversion is an acute obstetric emergency with a very different presentation, and postpartum blues do not cause fever or uterine pain.

distractorRationales.b: The option can leave infection progression untreated because it answers a lower-acuity part of the scenario. "Endometritis" fits the safer response to the cue "Postpartum fever with uterine tenderness and foul lochia points to endometritis, not routine recovery discomfort".
distractorRationales.c: This distractor shifts care toward misprioritization instead of infection or sepsis escalation. The stem cue "Uterine inversion is an acute obstetric emergency with a very different presentation" makes infection progression the active safety concern.
distractorRationales.d: The safer response addresses infection progression; "Expected afterpains" moves care toward reassurance before infection or sepsis escalation.

questions.takeaway: Postpartum fever with uterine tenderness and foul lochia points to endometritis, not routine recovery discomfort.
